item.title: Just wonder...
item.content:encoded: What will happen if a child makes a wish to SCP-3382 after ΩK?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It makes sense if she can only grant wishes to children whoa re terminally ill, or whose illness will become terminal due to inability to get treatment, therefore, the children have to be ill. The vehement response can be because that is simply how the wish granting works, and there is nothing that can be changed, so the children immediately become terminally ill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the idea is good but the writing is ham fisted in places.

The over-explanation of how the Foundation monitors online communities and that specific community leadership is a distraction.
The references to other SCPs are also distracting. Especially the explicit use of Santa.
Finally, why does this skip need to make billboards? Wouldn’t it be creepier if it randomly called children in hospitals that were just given news they were terminally ill?
This way the vindictive nature of the Cancer Genie can focus on adults? The test kids would thus be answering the phone under the assumption they were actually sick.

The writing of the Test Logs needs a lot of work. The Cancer Genie comes off as petty and cruel. Though I suspect the authors intention was to make her sympathetic. I wanted her to be sympathetic. So her unnecessary cruelty was jarring.

Finally, the example of the Warriors winning seems off given the example of bringing Grandpa back to life seems more innocent, more believable, and more off-putting if followed to its logical conclusion. Perhaps trade those around.

I think this article needs more work. But I clearly understand the story being told and that story is very intriguing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AGH this one tears me up so badly.

I so desperately want to like it and I do but at the same time AUGH.

Take your +1 before I reconsider ;~;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, it's short and clean and doesn't overstay its welcome, and your logs are interesting and have some cool implications. I especially liked the kid who "wants to see mummy again" and then immediately dies - that's some dark stuff right there.

My only real issue with this is the number of blackboxes everywhere. I can't see a single blackbox that you couldn't replace with the actual information with no issues whatsoever. Besides that though, I enjoyed this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually had 4040 initially, based on the classic 'How Dr Clef Saved Christmas' tale, but the tale was edited while I was drafting to avoid clashing with Series V. If your skip ends up on the mainlist I'll definitely look at replacing those black boxes, though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All around solid work. +1. I wanted to comment to inform you that I am currently planning out how to write Santa Claus as a mainlist SCP, and will have it done by the holiday season… so keep an eye out :).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this premise, and you executed it perfectly! I look forward to seeing more stuff from you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how this turns from a standard feel good skip (which is good on it’s own right) to something more cruel and malicious. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will live my life that the boy in the first experiment met Bob Saget.

This was good! I enjoyed the simplicity of the fact that this landline is just around to make sick kids feel good, but for some reason, will make those who attempt to extort it suffer, even if they're just kids, but why? Why would this SCP harm young children? Is there a backstory? Did some sort of World Destroying Event Occur? It'd be nice to know, but this is great! 87%

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mostly everything that the users above me said. Great concept, far-reaching enough to be classed as Keter but still containable.

Capitalize "addendum" and "test log" in some places; this is the only formatting quirk which I found.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thankyou for the feedback, and the upvote! I did define -B immediately after -A, but it could definitely be clearer. I'll relocate the designation to explicitly refer to the phone voice a little later in the body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to admit I was disappointed that billboards advertising "ill wishes" weren't connected to a hotline that cursed people. That said, I think this works; I was particularly impressed by the kid who wanted his parents back together. Like, it's not a simple matter to fix a family with magic.

One complaint is that -B is never defined. I can gather from context what it's supposed to be, and this would be a trivial matter to fix. +1

item.title: Re: Special thanks
item.content:encoded: I'm psyched to see this up and doing well. It's a killer idea, and your instincts are keen as heck. Now all you gotta do is do it again!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You got something pretty unique here and I can always appreciate that in an SCP

I think everyone's downvoting due to wikidot messing up and screwing up the articles format so I decided to fix that for you.

Otherwise, nice skip: +1

item.title: Special thanks
item.content:encoded:
This is my first SCP and it really wouldn't have been possible without the help of this great community. Special thanks to:
amnestic_protocol, for your amazing feedback, and giving me the courage to publish this.
Countless Skies for your great encouragement and guidance.
ResearcherKnochelman and DrAlewine for their forum feedback
Drecka for the invaluable fine-tooth comb assessment
And Lixerf, who took the mammoth task of tackling my first draft and helping refine it!
